name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ ۖ فَٱصْفَحِ ٱلصَّفْحَ ٱلْجَمِيلَ (٨٥)
```

```english
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom, and indeed the Hour is bound to come. So forbear with a graceful forbearance. (85)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨٦)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is the All-creator, the All-knowing. (86)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَثَانِى وَٱلْقُرْءَانَ ٱلْعَظِيمَ (٨٧)
```

```english
Certainly We have given you [the surah of] the seven oft-repeated verses and the great Quran. (87)
```

```arabic
لَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided to certain groups of them, and do not grieve for them, and lower your wing to the faithful, (88)
```

```arabic
وَقُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا ٱلنَّذِيرُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٨٩)
```

```english
and say, ‘I am indeed a manifest warner [of punishment from God],’ (89)
```

```arabic
كَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْمُقْتَسِمِينَ (٩٠)
```

```english
like that We sent down upon those who split into bands, (90)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عِضِينَ (٩١)
```

```english
who represented the Quran as magic. (91)
```

```arabic
فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْـَٔلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩٢)
```

```english
By your Lord, We will question them all (92)
```

```arabic
عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
concerning what they used to do. (93)
```

```arabic
فَٱصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٩٤)
```

```english
So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists. (94)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا كَفَيْنَـٰكَ ٱلْمُسْتَهْزِءِينَ (٩٥)
```

```english
Indeed We will suffice you against the deriders (95)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْعَلُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
—those who set up another deity besides Allah. Soon they will know! (96)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ (٩٧)
```

```english
Certainly We know that you become upset because of what they say. (97)
```

```arabic
فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٩٨)
```

```english
So celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate, (98)
```

```arabic
وَٱعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ ٱلْيَقِينُ (٩٩)
```

```english
and worship your Lord until certainty comes to you. (99)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses conclude with the objective of the preceding discourse, which is the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to openly proclaim what he is commanded, to adopt forgiveness, and to turn away from the polytheists (المشركين). He is told not to grieve over them nor to let his breast be straitened by what they say. Indeed, it is part of the True Decree (القضاء الحق) that people be recompensed for their deeds in this world and the Hereafter, especially on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), about which there is no doubt. It is the day that leaves no one behind and does not omit an atom’s weight of good or evil without attaching it to its doer. Thus, one should not despair over the disbelief of a disbeliever, for Allah is All-Knowing (عليم) of him and will recompense him; nor should one grieve over him, for preoccupation with Allah, the Glorified, is more important and more obligatory.

The Glorified has repeated His command to him to practice forgiveness and to turn away from those mockers—who were mentioned at the opening of the *Surah*—and to occupy himself with His glorification, His praise, and His worship (عبادته). He informed him that He has sufficed him against their evil, so let him occupy himself with what Allah has commanded him. With this, the *Surah* concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ ، وَإِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ** — **We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth, and indeed the Hour is bound to come.** (15:85). The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying "with the truth" (بالحق) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة); meaning that the creation of all of them is inseparable from the truth and is accompanied by it. Thus, creation has a goal (غاية) to which it shall return. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى** — **Indeed, the return is to your Lord.** (96:8). Were it not for that, it would have been a vain play (لعبا باطلا). The Exalted said: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ** — **We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth.** (44:38-39). And He said: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً** — **We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain.** (38:27). Evidence that "the truth" (الحق) here refers to that which stands in opposition to vain play is the concluding of the statement with His saying: "and indeed the Hour is bound to come" (وَإِنَّ السَّاعَةَ لَآتِيَةٌ), which is evident.

By this, the invalidity of what some have mentioned becomes clear—that "the truth" (الحق) means justice and equity (العدل والإنصاف), and that the preposition (بـ) denotes causality (السببية), meaning: "We did not create that except because of justice and equity on the Day of Recompense for deeds."

This is because there is no linguistic evidence for "the truth" in the verse meaning justice and equity. Furthermore, the meaning they mentioned would only be consistent if the preposition (بـ) meant the "lam" of purpose (لام الغرض) or accompaniment, rather than causality.

Similarly invalid is what some have mentioned—that "the truth" means wisdom (الحكمة), and that the first sentence "We did not create..." etc., refers to worldly punishment, while the second "and indeed the Hour is bound to come" refers to the punishment of the Hereafter. The meaning would then be: "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except as being clothed in truth and wisdom, such that the persistence of corruption and the establishment of evils are not compatible with it. Wisdom has necessitated the destruction of the likes of these people to repel their corruption and to guide those who remain toward righteousness; and indeed the Hour is bound to come, wherein vengeance will also be taken against the likes of these."

In this verse, there is a dispute between the proponents of compulsion (أصحاب الجبر) and the proponents of delegation (أصحاب التفويض), each group pulling the fire toward its own loaf. The proponents of compulsion used it as evidence that the acts of servants are created by Allah, because their deeds are among "whatever is between them," so they are created by Him.

The proponents of delegation used it as evidence that the acts of servants are not created by Him, but rather by themselves; for sins and ugly deeds are part of falsehood (الباطل), and if they were created by Him, they would be created "with the truth," and falsehood cannot be created with the truth.

The truth is that both arguments are invalid. The aspects of ugliness and sin in actions are non-existential (عدمية) qualities. For obedience and disobedience—like marriage and adultery, or consuming wealth lawfully or through falsehood, and the like—share the same essential act. They only differ as obedience or disobedience based on compliance with the command or violation of it, and violation is a non-existential aspect. Since this is the case, the attribution of the act to creation from the perspective of existence (الوجود) does not necessitate the attribution of the ugly or the sinful to it, for those are among its non-existential aspects. Thus, the act, in its non-existential aspect, is not part of "whatever is between the heavens and the earth" such that the verse would include it; nor is it, in its existential aspect, part of falsehood such that its creation would be the creation of falsehood with the truth.

Furthermore, necessity dictates the governance of the system of causes and effects (نظام العلل والمعلولات) in existence, and that the subsistence of the existence of a thing through another—such that it cannot be independent without it—is the criterion for attribution (الاتصاف). Thus, the one characterized by obedience, disobedience, the beauty of an act, or its ugliness is the human being, not the One who created him and facilitated for him to do such-and-such. Just as the one characterized by blackness or whiteness is the body in which these two colors subsist, not the One who brought it into existence.

We have fully covered the discussion on this topic in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — **and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors** (2:26) in the first volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ** — **So dispense a graceful pardon. Indeed your Lord is the All-Creator, the All-Knowing.** (15:85-86). It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "The *safh* (صفح) of a thing is its breadth and its side, like the *safhah* (surface) of the face, the *safhah* of the sword, and the *safhah* of the stone. *Al-Safh* (الصفح) is to abandon rebuke (التثريب), and it is more profound than *al-afw* (العفو) — *forgiveness*. Therefore, He said: **فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ** — **So pardon and be indulgent until Allah ordains His edict.** (2:109). A person may pardon (يعفو) but not dispense a graceful pardon (يصفح). The Exalted said: **فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ** — **So disregard them and say, ‘Peace!’** (43:89); **فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ** — **So dispense a graceful pardon** (15:85); and **أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنْكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحاً** — **Shall We ignore you and withdraw the Reminder?** (43:5).

And 'I dispensed a graceful pardon to him' (صفحت عنه) means I turned a beautiful side to him, turning away from his sin; or I met his side while being distant from him; or I bypassed the page (*safhah*) in which his sin was recorded in the book to another, from the expression 'I leafed through (*tasaffah-tu*) the book.' His saying: 'Indeed the Hour is bound to come, so dispense a graceful pardon' is a command to him (peace be upon him) to lighten the burden of the disbelief of whoever disbelieves, as He said: **وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ** — **Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting.** (16:127). And *al-musafahah* (handshaking) is the meeting of the surfaces (*safhah*) of the hands." [End of quote].

There will follow in the section on narrations (الرواية) the interpretation (تفسير) of Imam Ali (peace be upon him) of *al-safh* as forgiveness without reproach.

His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon" (فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ) is a derivation from what preceded it. That is: since creation is with the truth, and there is a day in which they will be held to account and recompensed—about which there is no doubt—then do not occupy yourself with the denial and mockery you see from them. Forgive them without falling into reproach, dispute, or argument with them. For your Lord, who created you and created them, is All-Knowing (عليم) of your state and their state, and ahead of them is a day they cannot escape.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying: "Indeed your Lord is the All-Creator, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ الْخَلَّاقُ الْعَلِيمُ) is the justification (تعليل) for His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon."

These verses surrounding His saying: "So proclaim what you are commanded" (فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ) are a consolation (تسلية) for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a soothing of his soul, so that His saying: "So proclaim what you are commanded" may find its proper place. You have learned at the beginning of the *Surah* that the primary objective of it is the command to announce the call (الدعوة), and you have also learned by reflecting on the previous verses that they are arranged to lead into consoling him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for the harm, insult, and mockery he faced from his people, and to transition from that to the required command.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْناكَ سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ** — **Certainly We have given you the seven of the oft-repeated [verses] and the great Quran.** (15:87). The "Seven Oft-Repeated" (السبع المثاني) is *Surah al-Hamd*, according to what has been interpreted in several narrations transmitted from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). Therefore, no heed should be paid to what some have mentioned: that they are the seven long *Surahs* (السبع الطوال); or what others mentioned: that they are the seven *Surahs* beginning with *Ha-Mim* (الحواميم السبع); or what has been said: that they are seven scrolls from the scrolls revealed to the prophets. There is no evidence for any of these from the wording of the Book nor from the Sunnah.

There has been much disagreement regarding His saying: "of the oft-repeated" (مِنَ الْمَثانِي) as to whether "of" (مِنَ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض) or clarification (التبيين), and regarding the derivation of the word *al-mathani* (المثاني) and the reason for it being so named.

What ought to be said—and Allah knows best—is that "of" (مِنَ) denotes partitivity. For the Glorified has named all the verses of His Book *mathani* (oft-repeated) when He said: **كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ** — **a Book, consistent in its parts, and oft-repeated, whereat quiver the skins of those who fear their Lord** (39:23). The verses of *Surah al-Hamd* are among them; thus, they are some of the *mathani*, not all of them.

It appears that *al-mathani* (المثاني) is the plural of *mathniyyah* (مثنية), a passive participle from *al-thany* (الثني) meaning twisting, bending, or repeating. The Exalted said: **يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ** — **they fold up their breasts** (11:5). The Quranic verses were named *mathani* because some of them clarify the state of others and bend or incline toward them, as suggested by His saying: "a Book, consistent in its parts, and oft-repeated" (كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ), where He combined the Book being consistent (mutashabihan)—meaning its verses resemble one another—and its verses being *mathani*. In the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) describing the Quran: "**Some of it confirms the other**" (يصدق بعضه بعضا). And from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding it: "**Some of it speaks through the other, and some of it bears witness to the other**" (ينطق بعضه ببعض ويشهد بعضه على بعض). Or it is the plural of *mathna* (مثنى) in the sense of repetition and recurrence, as a metaphor for the clarification of some verses by others.

Perhaps this is sufficient and renders unnecessary the various meanings they have mentioned, such as in *al-Kashshaf* and its glosses, *al-Majma'*, *Ruh al-Ma'ani*, and others. For example, their saying: it is from *tathniyah* (doubling) or *thany* in the sense of repetition and recurrence; the verses of the Quran were named *mathani* because the meanings are repeated in them. Or their saying: *al-Fatihah* was named *mathani* because of the obligation to recite it twice in every prayer, or because it is repeated in every unit (*rak'ah*) with what is recited after it of the Quran, or because many of its words are repeated like "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن), "the Most Merciful" (الرحيم), "You" (إياك), "the path" (الصراط), and "upon them" (عليهم). Or because it was revealed twice, once in Mecca and once in Medina; or because of the praise (ثناء) of Allah it contains; or because Allah excepted it and reserved it for this nation and did not reveal it to past nations, as in the narration; among other views mentioned in the commentaries.

In His saying: "the seven of the oft-repeated and the great Quran" (سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ), there is an exaltation of the matter of *al-Fatihah* and the Quran that is not hidden. As for the Quran, it is due to its being described from the realm of greatness and majesty as "great" (العظيم). As for *al-Fatihah*, it is due to its being expressed as an undescribed indefinite noun "seven" (سَبْعاً), which contains an indication of the greatness of its value and the majesty of its status that is not hidden, and it has been placed in parallel with the Great Quran, even though it is a part of it.

The verse—as has become clear—is in the context of conferring a favor (الامتنان). Moreover, because it occurs in the context of the call to forgiveness and turning away, it implies that in this greatest gift, which contains the realities of divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) that guide to every perfection and happiness by Allah's permission, there is that which should enable you to dispense a graceful pardon, to occupy yourself with your Lord, and to immerse yourself in His obedience.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ** — **Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them with...** until His saying: **الْمُبِينُ** — **the manifest [warner]** (15:88-89). These two verses are in the position of clarifying the "graceful pardon" (الصفح الجميل) that was previously commanded. Therefore, the speech was brought in the form of a new sentence (استئناف).

Mentioned in them are four directives: two negative and two positive. His saying: "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them with" (لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ)—extending the eyes toward what they have been provided with of the splendor of the life of this world is a metaphor for transgressing by not limiting one's sight to the blessing Allah has given. What is meant by "groups" (أزواجاً) are pairs of men and women, or categories of people such as the idolaters, the Jews, the Christians, and the Magians. The meaning is: do not look beyond the manifest and hidden blessings We have bestowed upon you toward what We have provided to a few pairs or categories of the disbelievers.

Perhaps some have taken His saying: "Do not extend your glance" (لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ) as a metaphor for prolonging and sustaining the gaze. You know that the objective, in any case, is the prohibition of desire, inclination, and heart-attachment to the goods of life in their hands, such as wealth, power, and fame. What serves as a metaphor for that is the prohibition of the very act of looking at it, not just its prolongation. This is supported by what we will quote from the verse in *Surah al-Kahf*.

His saying: **وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ** — **and do not grieve for them** (15:88); meaning, on account of their persistence in denial and mockery, and their insistence on not believing in you.

His saying: **وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **and lower your wing to the believers** (15:88). They said: it is a metaphor for humility (التواضع) and gentleness of side. Its origin is that when a bird wants to gather its chicks to itself, it spreads its wing over them and then lowers it for them.

What they mentioned, although it can be supported by other verses such as His saying: **فَبِما رَحْمَةٍ مِنَ اللهِ لِنْتَ لَهُمْ** — **It is by Allah’s mercy that you are gentle to them** (3:159) and His saying describing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ** — **clement and merciful to the faithful** (9:128); however, what occurs in the parallel of this verse, by which "lowering the wing" can be interpreted, is the restraining of the soul with the believers. This is suitable to be a metaphor for gathering the believers to himself and limiting his concern to associating with them, nurturing them, and disciplining them with the discipline of Allah; or a metaphor for staying with them and remaining among them without parting, just as a bird, when it lowers its wing, does not fly or depart. The Exalted said: **وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ وَلا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** — **Content yourself with those who pray to their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face, and do not let your eyes pass beyond them, desiring the adornment of the life of this world.** (18:28).

His saying: **وَقُلْ إِنِّي أَنَا النَّذِيرُ الْمُبِينُ** — **And say, ‘I am indeed the manifest warner.’** (15:89); meaning: I have no claim except that I am a warner (نذير) warning you of the punishment of Allah, the Glorified, a manifest one (مبين) clarifying for you what you need to have clarified. Beyond that, I have no authority in the matter.

These four matters—namely, abandoning desire for the goods of the life of this world in their hands, abandoning grief over them when they disbelieve and mock, lowering the wing to the believers, and declaring that he is a manifest warner—constitute the "graceful pardon" (الصفح الجميل) that befits the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). If one of them were dropped, the matter would be deficient.

From this, it becomes clear that the saying of some—that His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon" (فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ) was abrogated (منسوخ) by the "Verse of the Sword"—is groundless. For this pardon which the verse commands and which His saying "Do not extend your glance" interprets, remains in its legal force and validity even after the revelation of the Verse of the Sword. Thus, there is no reason to attribute abrogation to it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **كَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الْمُقْتَسِمِينَ الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ** — **[We will punish them] just as We sent down [punishment] on the dividers, who have broken the Quran into parts.** (15:90-91). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "*Idin* (عِضِينَ) is the plural of *idah* (عضة), and its root is *udwah* (عضوة), so the *waw* was dropped, and thus it was pluralized as *idin* with the *nun*, just as it is said *izwah* and *izun*, the root being *izwah*. *Al-ta'diyah* (التعضية) is division (التفريق), derived from *al-a'da'* (الأعضاء) — *limbs/parts*. It is said: 'I divided (*addaytu*) the thing,' meaning I separated it and partitioned it. Ru'bah said: 'And the religion of Allah is not something to be divided (*bi-l-mu'addi*).'" [End of the relevant portion].

His saying: "just as We sent down on the dividers" (كَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الْمُقْتَسِمِينَ)—the context is not devoid of indication that it is connected to something omitted, suggested by His saying: "And say, ‘I am indeed the manifest warner’"; meaning: [I warn you] of a sent-down punishment that will descend upon you, just as We sent it down upon the dividers. What is meant by "the dividers" (المقتسمين) are those whom He describes afterward: "who have broken the Quran into parts" (الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ). According to what has been reported in narrations, they were a group of the disbelievers of Quraysh who partitioned the Quran into parts; they said: "sorcery," and they said: "fables of the ancients," and they said: "a fabrication." They dispersed at the entrances of the roads to Mecca during the pilgrimage season to turn people away from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as will follow in the discussion on narrations, if Allah wills.

It has been said that His saying: "just as We sent down" (كَما أَنْزَلْنا) is connected to what preceded it of His saying: "Certainly We have given you the seven of the oft-repeated"; meaning: We sent down the Quran upon you just as We sent down upon the dividers. What is meant by "the dividers" are the Jews and Christians who divided the Quran into parts and portions, saying: "We believe in some and disbelieve in some."

The flaw in this is that the *Surah* is Meccan, revealed in the early days of the Mission (البعثة), and Islam had not yet been afflicted by the Jews and Christians to that extent. Their saying: **آمِنُوا بِالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَجْهَ النَّهارِ وَاكْفُرُوا آخِرَهُ** — **Believe in what has been sent down to the faithful at the beginning of the day, and disbelieve at its end** (3:72) is among what the Jews said after the Migration (الهجرة), as is similar to it. The evidence for what we have mentioned is the context of the verses.

It has also been said: they were named "dividers" (مقتسمين) because they divided the prophets of Allah and the books revealed to them, believing in some and disbelieving in some. This is refuted by the fact that the following verse interprets "the dividers" as those who "have broken the Quran into parts," not as those who differentiated between the prophets of Allah or between His books.

Thus, it appears that the two verses mention a group who rose up in the early days of the Mission to extinguish the light of the Quran and partitioned it into parts to bar people from the way of Allah, so Allah sent down punishment upon them and destroyed them. They are the ones mentioned in the two verses. Then Allah mentions the outcome of their affair by His saying: **فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ عَمَّا كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — **By your Lord, We will surely question them all concerning what they used to do.** (15:92-93).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **So proclaim what you are commanded, and turn away from the polytheists.** (15:94). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "*Al-sad'* (الصدع), *al-farq* (الفرق), and *al-fasl* (الفصل) are synonyms. One 'proclaims the truth' (*sada'a bi-l-haqq*) when he speaks it openly." [End of quote].

The verse is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded. It is appropriate for it to be a derivation because it is, in reality, the objective of the *Surah*. That is: if the matter is as mentioned, and you have been commanded to dispense a graceful pardon, and you were a warner of Our punishment just as We sent it down upon the dividers, then manifest the word of truth and announce the call.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: **إِنَّا كَفَيْناكَ الْمُسْتَهْزِئِينَ** — **Indeed We will suffice you against the mockers** (15:95) is in the position of justification (تعليل) for His saying: "So proclaim..." etc., as the speech suggests, or it indicates that these mockers are the "dividers" mentioned before. The meaning of the verse is: if the matter is as We have mentioned, and you were a warner of Our punishment just as We sent it down upon the dividers, then "proclaim what you are commanded" and announce the call and manifest the truth, "and turn away from the polytheists, indeed We"—meaning because We—"will suffice you against the mockers" by sending down punishment upon them, and they are "those who set up another god besides Allah; soon they will know." (15:96).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ بِما يَقُولُونَ** — **We certainly know that your breast is straitened by what they say.** (15:97). He returned a second time to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) grief and the straitening of his breast from their mockery out of increased concern for consoling him, soothing his soul, and strengthening his spirit. The Glorified has done this frequently in His speech, especially in the Meccan *Surahs*, due to the severity of the matter upon him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَكُنْ مِنَ السَّاجِدِينَ وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ** — **So celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate, and worship your Lord until the Certainty comes to you.** (15:98-99). The Glorified counseled him to practice glorification (التسبيح), praise (التحميد), prostration (السجدة), and worship (العبادة) or the continuation of servitude (العبودية), deriving that from the straitening of his breast by what they say. In this, there is seeking of assistance against sorrow and calamity. He had commanded him in the previous verses to practice pardon and patience (الصبر). The command to be patient is also inferred from His saying: "and worship your Lord until the Certainty comes to you," for its apparent meaning is the command to be patient in servitude until a certain time. By this, the content of the speech becomes close to His saying regarding the repelling of hardships and enduring bitter events: **اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلاةِ** — **Take recourse in patience and prayer** (2:153).

By this, it is supported that what is meant by "those who prostrate" (الساجدين) are those who pray (المصلون), and that he was commanded to perform prayer (الصلاة), which was named "prostration" (سجود) by naming it after its most excellent part. What is meant by glorification and praise would be the verbal form of them, such as saying "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله) and "Praise be to Allah" (الحمد لله) or their equivalent. Yes, if what was meant by "prayer" in the verse of *al-Baqarah* was turning toward Allah, the Glorified, it would be possible for what is meant by glorification and praise—or by them and prostration—to be the linguistic meaning, which is declaring Him transcendent (تنزيهه) above what they say, praising Him for the blessings He has bestowed upon him, and humbling oneself before Him with the humility of servitude.

**As for His saying**: "and worship your Lord until the Certainty comes to you" (وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ)—if what is meant by it is the command to perform worship (العبادة), it would be like an interpretation of the previous verse. If what is meant is the adoption of servitude (العبودية)—as is the apparent context, especially the context of the previous verses commanding pardon and turning away, which necessitate patience—then by the context of its being restricted by His saying: "until the Certainty comes to you," it is a command to follow the path of submission, obedience, and fulfilling the requirements of servitude.

Accordingly, what is meant by the "coming of Certainty" (إتيان اليقين) is the arrival of the appointed term and the descent of death (الموت), by which the Unseen (الغيب) is transformed into the witnessed (الشهادة) and the report becomes ocular vision (عيانا). This is supported by the derivation of what preceded of His saying: "So dispense a graceful pardon" from His saying: "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth, and indeed the Hour is bound to come." For it is, in reality, a command to forgive and be patient with what they say because they have a day in which Allah will take vengeance upon them and recompense them for their deeds. Thus, the meaning of the verse is: continue in servitude, be patient in obedience, refrain from disobedience, and endure the bitterness of what they say until death overtakes you and the realm of Certainty (عالم اليقين) descends upon you, and you witness what your Lord does with them.

In the expression like His saying: "until the Certainty comes to you," there is also an indication of this. For the concern in it is that Certainty is seeking him and will overtake him, so let him worship his Lord until it overtakes him and he reaches it. This is the realm of the Hereafter, which is the realm of general certainty regarding what is behind the veil, rather than the certain belief (الاعتقاد اليقيني) that might be attained through reflection or worship.

By this, the invalidity of what has sometimes been said becomes clear—that the verse indicates the lifting of religious obligation (التكليف) upon the attainment of certainty. This is because the one addressed by it is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and many verses from the Book of Allah have indicated that he is among those who possess certainty (الموقنين), that he is upon clear insight (بصيرة), that he is upon a clear proof (بينة) from his Lord, that he is infallible (معصوم), and that he is guided by the guidance of Allah, the Glorified, among other things. This is in addition to what we have already presented regarding the verse's indication that what is meant by "Certainty" is death.

We shall dedicate a rational discussion to the continuity of religious obligation after concluding the discussion on narrations, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

## (Discussion on Narrations)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn al-Najjar narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: **فَاصْفَحِ الصَّفْحَ الْجَمِيلَ** — "So dispense a graceful pardon." He said: "**Pleasure without reproach.**"

In *al-Majma'*, it is related from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) that "graceful pardon" is forgiveness without reproach.

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain of transmission from Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Faddal, from his father, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "**Forgiveness without reproach.**"

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the 'Seven Oft-Repeated' and the 'Great Quran'—is it *Fatihat al-Kitab*? He said: '**Yes.**' I said: 'Is *Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim* among the seven?' He said: '**Yes, it is the best of them.**'"

**I say**: This is narrated through Shi'i paths from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and more than one of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them); and through Sunni paths from Ali and several companions such as Umar ibn al-Khattab, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Ibn Abbas, Ubayy ibn Ka'b, Abu Hurayrah, and others.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani narrated in *al-Awsat* from Ibn Abbas, who said: "A man asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: 'What do you say about the saying of Allah: **كَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الْمُقْتَسِمِينَ**?' He said: '**The Jews and the Christians.**' He asked: '**الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ**?' He said: '**They believed in some and disbelieved in some.**'"

**I say**: You have already learned that the content of this narration is not consistent with the *Surah* being Meccan.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding His saying: **الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ**, they both said: "**They are Quraysh.**"

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from Abdullah ibn Ali al-Halabi, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: '**The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained in Mecca after the revelation came from Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, for thirteen years—concealing himself for three years of them, fearful and not appearing—until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded him to proclaim what he was commanded; then he manifested the call.**'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Ubaydah that Abdullah ibn Mas'ud said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) continued to conceal himself until there was revealed: **فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ** (So proclaim what you are commanded); then he and his companions came out."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Ali al-Halabi, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "**The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained hidden in Mecca for years—not appearing, with Ali and Khadijah with him—then Allah commanded him to proclaim what he was commanded. So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) appeared and began presenting himself to the tribes of the Arabs; whenever he came to them, they said: 'A liar! Move away from us.'**"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Aban ibn Uthman al-Ahmar, in a *marfu'* narration, he said: "**The mockers were five from Quraysh: al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah al-Makhzumi, al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, al-Harith ibn Hanzalah, al-Aswad ibn Abd Yaghuth ibn Wahb al-Zuhri, and al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib ibn Asad. When Allah said: 'Indeed We will suffice you against the mockers,' the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) knew that He had disgraced them, so Allah caused them to die the worst of deaths.**"

**I say**: It was narrated by al-Saduq in *al-Ma'ani* with his chain from Aban. He also narrated therein, as did al-Tabarsi in *al-Ihtijaj*, from Musa ibn Ja'far, from his fathers, from Ali (peace be upon him), what is in this meaning—and it is a long *hadith* containing the details of the destruction of each of these five, may Allah curse them. The fact that the mockers were five from Quraysh is narrated from Ali and from Ibn Abbas along with the cause of their destruction.

The narrations, nonetheless, differ through Sunni paths regarding their number, their names, and the causes of their destruction. What the reports of both groups agree upon is what we have presented.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh and al-Daylami narrated from Abu al-Darda: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: '**It has not been revealed to me that I should be a merchant, nor that I should amass wealth in abundance; but it has been revealed to me: "So celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate, and worship your Lord until the Certainty comes to you."**'"

**I say**: What is in this meaning was also narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Mas'ud from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In it, al-Bukhari and Ibn Jarir narrated from Umm al-Ala: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered upon Uthman ibn Maz'un after he had died, and I said: 'The mercy of Allah be upon you, O Abu al-Sa'ib; my testimony for you is that Allah has honored you.' He said: '**And what makes you know that Allah has honored him? As for him, the Certainty (اليقين) has come to him; I indeed hope for good for him.**'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Hafs ibn Ghiyath, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: '**Indeed, whoever is patient is patient for a little while, and whoever is anxious is anxious for a little while.**'"

Then he said: "**You must practice patience in all your affairs, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent Muhammad and commanded him to practice patience and gentleness, saying: 'And be patient with what they say, and keep away from them in a graceful manner. Leave Me to deal with the deniers, the possessors of ease and plenty.' And the Blessed and Exalted said: 'Repel [evil] with what is best. [If you do so,] behold, he between whom and you was enmity will be as though he were a sympathetic friend. But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed.'**"

"**So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was patient until they afflicted him with great matters and accused him of them, and his breast was straitened. Then Allah said: 'We certainly know that your breast is straitened by what they say. So celebrate the praise of your Lord and be among those who prostrate.'**"

## (Philosophical Discussion on the Nature of the Existence of Religious Obligation and its Continuity)

It has preceded during the discussions on Prophethood and the manner of the emergence of heavenly laws in this book that every species among the types of existents has a perfective goal (غاية كمالية) toward which it is directed, striving for it and seeking it through an existential movement (حركة وجودية) suitable to its existence. It does not rest until it attains it, unless a hindering obstacle prevents it, causing it to fail before reaching its goal—like a tree that stops growing and developing before reaching its end due to afflictions that befall it. It has also preceded that deprivation from reaching goals only occurs in specific individuals of the species, whereas for the species as a species, this is inconceivable.

Furthermore, the human being is an existential species that has an existential goal which he cannot attain except through civil society (الاجتماع المدني), as evidenced by the equipping of his existence with that which makes him dependent on others of his kind—such as masculinity and femininity, emotions and sensations, and the multiplicity and accumulation of needs.

The realization of this association and the formation of human society requires the individuals of the society to have rulings and laws (أحكام وقوانين) through whose respect and implementation their scattered affairs are organized, their necessary differences are removed, each of them stands in his appropriate position, and through which he achieves his happiness and existential perfection. These practical rulings and laws are, in reality, emanated from the needs called for by the specificity of human existence and his particular creation, with its physical and spiritual equipment, just as the specificity of his existence and creation is linked to the specificities of the causes and reasons that constitute the existence of the human being from the general cosmos.

This is the meaning of religion being primordial (فطريا)—that is, it is a collection of rulings and laws toward which human existence points according to the creative order (التكوين). Or if you wish, say: they are norms (سنن) demanded by the general cosmos. If they are established, they rectify society and bring individuals to their goal in existence and their required perfection; and if they are abandoned and invalidated, they corrupt the human world and conflict with the general cosmos in its system.

These rulings and laws—whether they are social-transactional (معاملية اجتماعية) by which the state of society is rectified and its unity gathered, or devotional (عبادية) by which the human being reaches his ultimate perfection in knowledge and righteousness within a righteous society—must all be received by the human being through the path of divine Prophethood and heavenly revelation, and nothing else.

By these past principles, it becomes clear that divine obligation (التكليف الإلهي) accompanies the human being as long as he lives in this worldly life, whether he is inherently deficient and has not yet reached existential perfection, or is perfect in knowledge and action. As for the deficient, it is obvious. As for the perfect, it is because the meaning of his perfection is that there should be established for him, in the aspects of knowledge and action, virtuous dispositions (ملكات فاضلة) from which proceed transactional acts that suit and rectify society, and he becomes capable of perfect knowledge and the performance of devotional acts suitable to that knowledge, as necessitated by the Divine Care (العناية الإلهية) that guides the human being to his happiness.

It is well known that permitting the lifting of obligation from the perfect human being is inseparable from permitting his deviation from the rulings and laws. In what pertains to transactions, this would necessitate the corruption of society, which Divine Care rejects. In what pertains to devotions, it would necessitate the deviation of the dispositions from their effects. For actions are preparatory preliminaries for the attainment of dispositions as long as they have not been attained; and once they are attained, those actions become their effects, proceeding from them in a manner that does not fail.

From here, the invalidity of what might be imagined becomes clear—that the purpose of obligation is the perfecting of the human being and bringing him to his existential goal, so if he becomes perfect, there is no meaning for the continuation of obligation.

The aspect of invalidity: the deviation of the human being from divine obligation—even if he is perfect—in transactions corrupts society, and in that is the invalidation of Divine Care for the species. In devotions, it necessitates the deviation of dispositions from their effects, which is not permissible; and if it were permissible, it would involve the invalidation of the disposition and also the invalidation of Divine Care. Yes, between the perfect human being and others, there is a difference in the proceeding of actions: the perfect one is protected from violation due to the firmly rooted disposition, unlike the one who is not perfect. And Allah is the One whose help is sought.
